Negative Appraisals.value: There is something wrong with me as a person
I feel isolated and set apart from others
Somebody else would not have gotten into this situation

Maladaptive Strategies.value: I distract myself with something else
I detach myself from the memories
I dwell on how the event could have been prevented

Threat.value: Having strong physical reactions when something reminded you of the stressful experience
Taking too many risks or doing things that could cause you harm

vignette:
He is a 78-year-old divorced South American man who presents with severe post-traumatic stress symptoms following an accident, with a PCL-5 score of 70 indicating marked distress. In day-to-day life, he remains on guard in situations that remind him of violence, especially when he hears gunshot-like noises or comes across related smells. These moments quickly bring a harsh view of himself to the foreground. He speaks of feeling that there is something wrong with him as a person, and of being fundamentally different from others, with a strong sense that somebody else would not have gotten into the situation he did.

These appraisals weigh heavily on him and shape much of his daily functioning. He frequently feels isolated and set apart from those around him, and this sense of separateness sits alongside a tendency to dwell on how the event could have been prevented. He also tries to distract himself with other activities and to detach from the memories when they intrude, though these efforts appear only partly effective. At times he acts in risky ways or does things that could cause him harm, suggesting a broader pattern of poor self-protection.

His sense of threat remains high. When reminded of the stressful experience, he has strong physical reactions, and he describes feeling jumpy and easily startled. These responses keep him in a state of tension and make ordinary environments feel unsafe. The combination of heightened alarm and negative self-judgment appears to sustain his distress and narrow his world.

Overall, his presentation is consistent with a cycle in which reminders of violence, gunshot noise, and related smells intensify harsh beliefs about himself, which in turn feed his attempts to push the experience away, his ongoing sense of danger, and his withdrawal from others. The result is a lonely and exhausting pattern that has taken a toll on his confidence, relationships, and day-to-day stability.
